title: Ring of Gyges
speaker: Glaucon
argument_context: Glaucon challenges Socrates to show why justice is valued beyond reputation and external reward.
analysis_prompts: What does invisibility remove from the social situation?
What does the challenge ask Socrates to prove about justice?
non_claims: not evidence that every person is inherently unjust

title: Civic foundation story and metals
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: The story appears in a discussion of guardians and civic formation in the proposed city.
analysis_prompts: Which political work is the story asked to perform?
How does the surrounding discussion constrain a reading of the story?
non_claims: not a justification for ranking living people by supposed natural metals

title: Cave
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: The image develops a discussion of education, knowledge, turning the soul, and the philosopher's return to the city.
analysis_prompts: How does the image stage change in what is taken as real?
Why does the return to the cave matter politically?

title: Myth of Er
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: The narrative closes the dialogue's long inquiry into justice, choice, and the formation of a life.
analysis_prompts: How are choice and responsibility staged in the narrative?
How does the closing narrative relate to the prior argument about justice?
non_claims: not a deterministic horoscope of a future life

title: Aristophanes' divided humans
speaker: Aristophanes
argument_context: Aristophanes gives one speech among several speeches about eros; it precedes Socrates' report of Diotima.
analysis_prompts: What does the comic story explain about longing and wholeness?
How does its speaker and genre differ from Diotima's account?
non_claims: not a biological account of sex or gender

title: Diotima's account of eros
speaker: Socrates reporting Diotima
argument_context: Socrates recounts a teaching attributed to Diotima during the dialogue's sequence of speeches on eros.
analysis_prompts: How does the reported teaching relate desire to beauty and generation?
What changes when the account is treated as reported speech?
non_claims: not interchangeable with Aristophanes' comic speech

title: Winged chariot and soul
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: The image belongs to a speech about soul, desire, divine procession, recollection, and love.
analysis_prompts: What does the image make visible about divided desire and guidance?
Which claims are image-language rather than modern psychology?
non_claims: not a literal diagram of the brain

title: Theuth and writing
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: The story appears in a discussion of writing, memory, speech, and dialectic.
analysis_prompts: What distinction between memory and external reminders is being explored?
Why should the story be read with the surrounding discussion of dialectic?
non_claims: not a simple ban on writing
not a prediction about every later technology

title: Afterlife geography
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: The narrative comes near the end of the dialogue's treatment of the soul and philosophical preparation for death.
analysis_prompts: How does the narrative connect moral formation with the dialogue's treatment of soul?
What is lost if its geography is read as a travel guide?

title: Post-mortem judgment
speaker: Socrates
argument_context: Socrates uses the narrative while arguing about justice, punishment, and rhetoric with Callicles.
analysis_prompts: How does judgment without social trappings illuminate the dialogue's concern with justice?
How does the story differ from evidence in a legal case?
non_claims: not courtroom evidence of an afterlife
not a description verified by observation

title: Age of Kronos and cosmic reversal
speaker: Eleatic Stranger
argument_context: The Stranger develops a cosmological story while distinguishing kinds of rule and political expertise.
analysis_prompts: What contrast between divine and human care does the reversal frame?
Why is a cosmic sequence not automatically a historical chronology?

title: Atlantis frame
speaker: Critias
argument_context: Critias presents a story about ancient Athens and Atlantis as the dialogue turns toward an account of an ideal city and its action; the story continues in the unfinished Critias.
analysis_prompts: How does the frame relate political memory to the ideal city discussion?
What is the difference between a narrative's setting and archaeological evidence?
non_claims: not evidence for a lost continent

title: Cosmological account
speaker: Timaeus
argument_context: Timaeus gives an account of cosmic order that is framed by the dialogue's distinctions between being, becoming, and likely account.
analysis_prompts: Which assumptions about order and explanation structure the account?
Why should its cosmology be read in its ancient philosophical context rather than as current science?
non_claims: not a substitute for astronomy or physics
